title: SCAF folds?
gist: The near decade-long Franco-German-Spanish SCAF/NGWS next-generation fighter program has collapsed after Berlin ended participation, undone by industrial rivalry between Airbus and Dassault; this reshapes European combat-air alignment, boosts likely German F-35 orders, sustains Rafale and Eurofighter backlogs, and opens GCAP/Sweden realignment possibilities.

themes.theme: Collapse of SCAF/NGF and its cause

insights.claim: Germany has decided to end participation in the Franco-German Future Combat Air System (SCAF)/Next-Generation Weapon System (NGWS), effectively collapsing the near decade-long Franco-German-Spanish next-generation fighter project.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Removes a major European next-gen fighter program from the pipeline, redirecting tens of billions in future combat-air spend.
insights.quote: A near decade-long Franco–German–Spanish project to jointly develop a New Generation Fighter appears to have fallen apart, with Berlin deciding to end its participation.

insights.claim: The SCAF/NGWS program, born of a 2017 Berlin-Paris political impetus that Spain joined in 2019, unravelled because of an inability to resolve competing industrial imperatives between Airbus and Dassault.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Industrial work-share rivalry, not a US purchase, killed the program; a structural risk for any future European multinational defense JV.
insights.quote: the Franco-German Future Combat Air System (SCAF)/Next-Generation Weapon System (NGWS) has unravelled because of an inability to resolve competing industrial imperatives between Airbus and Dassault.
insights.locator: Born out of a political impetus

insights.claim: The New Generation Fighter, the core of SCAF, was aimed at succeeding the Dassault Rafale and Eurofighter Typhoon with entry into service planned for some time in the 2040s.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Loss of a 2040s replacement program extends the service life and order relevance of Rafale and Eurofighter.
insights.quote: The NGF was aimed at developing a successor to the Dassault Rafale and the Eurofighter Typhoon, with an entry into service planned for some time in the 2040s.
insights.locator: The NGF was aimed at developing a successor

insights.claim: Dassault has a substantial Rafale order backlog and will continue developing the aircraft, while Airbus's German subsidiary also has a Eurofighter backlog, though not as large.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Backlogs cushion both firms near-term; Dassault is relatively better positioned on order book.
insights.quote: Dassault has a substantial order backlog for the Rafale, and will continue to develop the aircraft; Airbus's German subsidiary also has a Eurofighter backlog, though not as large.
insights.locator: The NGF was aimed at developing a successor

insights.claim: A further German purchase of Lockheed Martin F-35A Lightning II aircraft was likely even before Berlin dropped NGF, and the decision bolsters the likelihood of an additional order, with the German Air Force currently operating 65 Tornado Interdictor Strike aircraft to be replaced.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Upside for Lockheed Martin F-35 orders from Germany; the SCAF collapse removes a competing domestic-program rationale.
insights.quote: An additional purchase of F-35As was likely even before Berlin dropped NGF, and its decision only bolsters the likelihood of a further order.
insights.locator: The German Air Force now operates 65 Tornado

insights.claim: Germany's 2022 decision to buy the Lockheed Martin F-35A for the NATO dual-capable nuclear role drew French criticism that it would erode the rationale for SCAF/NGWS, though IISS attributes the eventual collapse to industrial antagonism rather than the US purchase.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Clarifies the failure driver as governance/work-share, relevant to assessing viability of GCAP and other JVs.
insights.quote: It was industrial antagonism, rather than a US purchase, that was the eventual cause of the collapse of NGF.
insights.locator: In addition to the industrial rivalry

themes.theme: Realignment options: GCAP, Sweden, Middle East

insights.claim: German participation in the Global Combat Air Programme (GCAP) is a possibility, but an immediate restructuring would be unwelcome if it introduced delays, with Japan having the most pressing requirement to replace the Mitsubishi F-2 in the mid-2030s.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Potential German entry could consolidate European next-gen fighter effort into GCAP but risks schedule friction; watch Tokyo's reaction.
insights.quote: Japan has the most pressing requirement of the current three GCAP partners, with the Mitsubishi F-2 needing to be replaced in the mid-2030s.

insights.claim: Delays in London's release of further GCAP funding have already unnerved Tokyo, where Italy, Japan and the UK participate as equal partners.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: GCAP funding reliability is a live risk; UK budget timing could affect program momentum and partner confidence.
insights.quote: Delays in London's release of further funding for the programme have already unnerved Tokyo.

insights.claim: France could look to Middle East partners following the collapse, with the UAE the largest Rafale customer in the region and ambitions to expand its defense aerospace base, and Saudi Arabia also attracting French interest.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Opens potential Gulf partnership/export avenues for Dassault's next-gen efforts.
insights.quote: The United Arab Emirates is the largest Rafale customer in the region and has ambitions to further develop its defence aerospace industrial base.
insights.locator: France will almost certainly continue supporting

insights.claim: German industry, led by Airbus, has tentatively proposed an industrial alliance called Team Gen 6 to the German government, as reported by the Financial Times, aiming to encourage continued support for a new combat aircraft.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Signals Airbus angling to retain German next-gen fighter work despite SCAF's collapse; watch for government endorsement.
insights.quote: German industry, led by Airbus, has tentatively proposed an industrial alliance to the German government, as reported by the Financial Times, to establish Team Gen 6.

insights.claim: IISS argues the Berlin-Paris split over a critical air-power capability undermines Europe's attempt to present a more cohesive picture on defense and security.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: The breakup is a setback for European defense-industrial integration narratives; watch knock-on effects for other joint programs.
insights.quote: Berlin and Paris's decision to part ways over a critical air-power capability hardly portrays such an image.
insights.locator: Irrespective of the reasons for the breakdown
